This session presents nine papers addressing architectural techniques for improving the efficiency of modern AI workloads, with particular emphasis on large language models (LLMs), diffusion models, and graph neural networks (GNNs). The contributions span both algorithmâarchitecture co-design and microarchitectural innovations. Collectively, the papers in this session illustrate the rapid evolution of AI hardware toward workload-specific acceleration, tighter algorithmâhardware co-design, and mechanisms that balance performance, energy efficiency, and deployability across both datacenter and edge environments.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr This session presents techniques that integrate learning with physics-based simulation, design space exploration, and circuit optimization. The papers explore graph learning, physics-informed neural networks, multi-physics modeling, and AI-driven search methods to improve scalability and accuracy across the physical design and device class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=616843">Adarsh Mittal</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>This session highlights advanced high-level approaches for improving the design, verification, and optimization at the system level, involving both software, the software/hardware interface, and the hardware design itself. Novel problems and opportunities for optimization appear as we move from components and IP-blocks to systems.  A central theme is the use of automation, machine learning, and standardized design abstractions to accelerate early developmentâfrom generating executable RTL and verification environments from high-level specifications to enabling early verification through enriched IP-XACT and PSS flows, as well as ML-driven design exploration such as die-size estimation and QoS optimization.</div><div This session highlights breakthrough advances - from fault tolerant in memory computing and superconducting logic to photonic matrix engines - alongside major progress in interconnect fabrics, 3D integration, and CXL enabled memory hierarchies. Showcasing innovations such as error resilient analog ECC, CAM based distance engines, wafer scale sparse matrix accelerators, and DTCO for 3â¯nm CPUs, these works demonstrate a compelling fusion of advanced device physics and system level architecture. Together, they signal a shift toward heterogeneous, hyper connected, high efficiency computing platforms where memory, logic, and communication co evolve to meet the demands of AI  and data centric workloads.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr class="agenda-item" e_utc="2026-07-27T17:43:00Z" psid="sess169" class="sess250 slots-slidedown" etypes="sstype108" style="display: none;"><td class="session-display" colspan="7"><div style="display: none;"><div class="info-section session-chairs-list-sect"><span class="info-label">Session Chair</span><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=464843">Yaguang Li</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>Generative AI is reshaping the landscape of analog design, a domain long constrained by manual expertise, limited automation, and complex layout-driven trade-offs. With the advent of generative AI models, the potential for automatic analog design has grown exponentially. These AI systems, acting as "design agents," have begun to demonstrate remarkable capabilities, offering the promise of automating analog design tasks (such as topology synthesis, sizing, and layout generation) that were once considered too intricate or too dependent on human intuition to be effectively automated. However, the shift from agent to true "design expert"âa system that can generate, optimize, and innovate in analog designârequires a deeper integration of domain knowledge and reasoning. This demands rethinking AI training, moving beyond simple generation to advanced learning methods that capture the underlying physical principles and trade-offs of analog design. This transformation is not just a technical evolution; it represents a shift in analog design, where AI moves from assisting with tasks like topology synthesis and layout generation to actively driving the design process, optimizing complex trade-offs and enabling fully automated workflows that were previously unattainable.<br><br>This special session features five talks that will highlight key advancements in AI-driven analog design automation, exploring how generative AI could evolve from assisting to potentially taking a more central role in the design process. The first talk reviews modern AI/ML methodologies for AMS/RF design and how they complement traditional expertise. The second introduces an LLM- and RL-based agentic workflow enabling automated analog/RF synthesis from spec to layout. The third presents an AI-native foundation-model framework for topology discovery, performance prediction, and intent-to-silicon flows. The fourth talk highlights hierarchical RL with LLM guidance for generating valid, high-quality analog circuit topologies. The final talk showcases an LLM-enhanced framework that automates and optimizes the analog design process, bridging design intent with layout generation.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr class="agenda-item" e_utc="2026-07-27T18:00:00Z" psid="sess250" s_utc="2026-07-27T17:30:00Z" ssid="SSSN101"><td class="presentation-time-td"><span class="left-cal-links"><span style="display: none;"><!-- cal-file sess250--SSSN101.ics --><a class="ical-link" href="/wp-content/plugins/linklings_wp_program/get_cal.php?id=sess250--SSSN101"></a></span><span class="agenda-add-button agenda-button clickable" name="add_to_agenda" role="switch" style="color: #cc0000; class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=584843">Karunveer Singh</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>This session presents an end-to-end view of how AI/ML techniques are influencing standard-cell and memory IP development. The topics cover AI-driven circuit optimization, from transistor-level cell sizing and memory tuning, to adaptive, high-sigma verification methodologies, characterization flows and automate frameworks that ensure signoff-quality. These works demonstrate how AI enables gains in power, performance, area and model reliability while reducing turnaround time in modern SoC design flows.				</div><div class="info-section Xing</a></div></span><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=721843">Ibrahim (Abe) Elfadel</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>This session explores the transformative intersection of generative AI and graph-based architectures in overcoming the âComplexity Wallâ of modern semiconductor design. By integrating in-context learning for circuit simulation, graph transformers for parasitic extraction, and diffusion-based surrogates for TCAD, these works replace traditional, computationally expensive numerical solvers with high-fidelity, scalable models. Additionally, breakthroughs in GPU-accelerated LU factorization and spatio-temporal GNNs demonstrate a paradigm shift toward real-time, structure-adaptive simulation. Together, these papers redefine the efficiency of the analog design loop, offering a roadmap for autonomous, high-performance physical modeling.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span Emphasizing hardware-software co-optimizations, these contributions reduce latency in data-intensive search operations, such as vector pruning, graph traversal, and embedding handling, to support scalable AI applications like point cloud processing and billion-scale datasets.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr none;"><div class="info-section session-chairs-list-sect"><span class="info-label">Session Chair</span><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=177643">Yervant Zorian</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>Advanced packaging technologies (2.5D, 3D IC, chiplets, fan-out, heterogeneous integration) are reshaping semiconductor integration, offering unprecedented improvements in performance, efficiency, and miniaturization. Test is a key challenge in advanced packaging due to limited physical access, exploding interconnct densities, mixed technologies, the need to ensure known good die, and the lack of standards. Test cost can be 30â50% of total manufacturing cost for advanced multi-die packages. The cost, reliability risks, and lack of visibility inside the package make test one of the biggest barriers to scalable chiplet and 3D IC adoption. This special session will spotlight test solutions for cutting-edge advanced packaging techniques, highlighting packaging architectures, industry test practices, and research breakthroughs. Speakers include experts from chip and package design (AMD), advanced foundry technology (TSMC), and academia.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span width: 30px;" tabindex="0" title="remove from Flagged" value="remove"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" stroke-width="1.5" id="icon_linklings_minus" class="icon-linklings-minus"><g fill="none" stroke="blue" stroke-miterlimit="10"><circle cx="12" cy="12" r="10.5"></circle><path d="m6.27 12h11.46"></path></g></svg></span></span></td></tr><tr class="sess115 slots-slidedown" etypes="sstype101" style="display: none;"><td class="session-display" colspan="7"><div style="display: none;"><div class="info-section session-chairs-list-sect"><span class="info-label">Session Chairs</span><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=204843">Shuo-Han Chen</a></div></span><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=725743">Pi-Cheng Hsiu</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>This session showcases advances that streamline computing from schedules to silicon, spanning optimization, acceleration, and validation. The talks explore Pareto-guided real-time DAG scheduling for latency reduction, followed by Pareto-optimal GEMM mapping on Versal ACAP for energy efficiency. The session then moves down the stack to address efficient FPGA debugging, expandable RISC-V register files for data-intensive workloads, and adaptive sparse tensor code generation for GPUs. To conclude, the session highlights pre-silicon assurance through SystemC fuzzing and transfer-enabled GPU power modeling, offering practical pathways to faster, greener, and safer embedded systems.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr class="agenda-item" e_utc="2026-07-27T20:42:00Z" psid="sess115" style="display: none;"><td class="session-display" colspan="7"><div style="display: none;"><div class="info-section session-chairs-list-sect"><span class="info-label">Session Chair</span><span class="session-chair-list"></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>The rapid evolution of AI has been fueled by massive GPU-based datacenters, enabling unprecedented model complexity and accuracy. However, this centralized paradigm faces critical limitationsâhigh latency, memory bandwidth bottlenecks, privacy concerns, and soaring energy demands. To democratize the revolutionizing power of AI, the next big leap is clear: bringing intelligence to the edge. Edge AI shifts inference from the cloud to local devices, unlocking real-time responsiveness, improved security, personalization, as well as sustainable scalability. This transformation needs to be optimally powered by heterogeneous compute platforms, where CPUs, GPUs, TPUs, and NPUs collaborate to deliver efficient AI and agentic workflow execution on resource-constrained environments. With this general vision, this session showcases four keynote talks followed by a panel discussion delving in to the advancements, opportunities, and challenges to enable token efficient AI at the Heterogeneous EDGE system.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr class="agenda-item" e_utc="2026-07-27T22:00:00Z" psid="sess293" s_utc="2026-07-27T20:30:00Z" ssid="ENGINV001"><td class="presentation-time-td"><span class="left-cal-links"><span style="display: none;"><!-- cal-file sess293--ENGINV001.ics --><a class="ical-link" href="/wp-content/plugins/linklings_wp_program/get_cal.php?id=sess293--ENGINV001"></a></span><span class="agenda-add-button agenda-button clickable" name="add_to_agenda" role="switch" style="color: #cc0000; width: 30px;" tabindex="0" title="add to Flagged" value="add"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" stroke-width="1.5" id="icon_linklings_plus" class="icon-linklings-plus"><g fill="red" stroke="white" stroke-miterlimit="10"><circle stroke="red" cx="12" cy="12" r="10.5"></circle><path d="m6.27 12h11.46"></path><path d="m12 We then examine the evolution of memory systems, featuring low-power SRAM subarrays and ECC integration for LPDDR6. This session further delves into system-level innovations, including ML-assisted analog calibration and cross-stage redundancy elimination for AIoT. From these talks, attendees will gain a comprehensive understanding of the cross-layer optimizations from circuits to software.<br></div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span This session presents advances spanning sparse tensor compilation and streaming accelerators, runtime-adaptive FPGA and heterogeneous platforms, and innovations in memory and buffering, from tile-level optimizations and fine-grained cache partitioning to HBM performance characterization. The session concludes with work on distributed buffering and scale-up network infrastructure, highlighting how architectural techniques across compute, memory, and system levels collectively enable high-performance, adaptable, and scalable acceleration.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr class="agenda-item" The papers span joint EMâIR modeling, robust simulation/solver techniques, and physics-informed learning that supports earlier screening and design-space exploration without disconnecting from electrical behavior. On the thermal side, the session highlights modular chiplet-oriented thermal modeling and cooling-aware prediction to surface hotspots and constraints earlier in the flow. Complementing these are constraint-aware P/G optimization and energy modeling for agile accelerator design. Together, these contributions emphasize practical, actionable integrity insights earlier, helping teams converge with fewer late surprises.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span This session highlights advances in AIâenabled verification, including generative modelâbased processor fuzzing for RISCâV, coordinated CPU fuzzing, and reinforcementâlearningâguided testâtime ensembles. Large language models are explored for automating UVM construction and assertionâguided protocol patching, reducing manual effort in testbench development and debug. These learningâbased techniques are integrated with scalable simulation, emulation, and FPGAâaccelerated infrastructures to maximize coverage and throughput. Together, the papers demonstrate how generative AI, RL, and LLMs are transforming verification workflows by enabling adaptive stimulus generation, intelligent repair, and autonomous validation at class="ical-link" href="/wp-content/plugins/linklings_wp_program/get_cal.php?id=sess251"></a></span></span><span class="agenda-add-button agenda-button clickable" name="add_to_agenda" role="switch" style="color: #cc0000; width: 30px;" tabindex="0" title="add to Flagged" value="add"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" stroke-width="1.5" class="sess251 slots-slidedown" etypes="sstype108" style="display: none;"><td class="session-display" colspan="7"><div style="display: none;"><div class="info-section session-chairs-list-sect"><span class="info-label">Session Chair</span><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=263843">Zhiyao Xie</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>Large Language Models (LLMs) and agentic workflows are rapidly reshaping the silicon design stackâfrom specification-to-RTL and verification to algorithm/tool evolution and physical layout closureâby writing, reading, and refactoring code across RTL (Verilog/SystemVerilog), Python, KLayout macros, C/C++, and Tcl-based flows. This special session brings together leaders from academia and industry to present concrete advances and a forward-looking roadmap for making code-capable LLMs trustworthy, reproducible, and production-ready for chip design.<br>We begin with front-end design where LLMs generate RTL and SystemVerilog testbenches from specs, propose debug strategies, and perform PPA-aware refactoring. Building on the Comprehensive Verilog Design Problems (CVDP) benchmark, we show how rigorous, community-vetted evaluation reveals capability gaps and drives progress across models and agentic systems. We then outline a roadmap toward tool-using AI agents that act as automated EDA R&D software engineersâmoving beyond expert users of tools to expert builders of toolsâwith documentation-grounded reasoning, literature-aware planning, and guarded code synthesis. Complementing this,<br>we present self-improving algorithmic infrastructure: an autonomous, correctness-gated framework that rewrites C/C++ solver code and optimizes scripts/flows in ABC, pointing to a new paradigm of closed-loop EDA tool evolution. On the path to manufacturability, we introduce a multimodal benchmark for DRC that fuses GDSII, compressed layout imagery, KLayout/Python scripts, and design rule metadataâenabling LLMs and agents to localize, explain, and emit repair edits and macros. Finally, we explore LLM-aided algorithm discovery for inverse design of superconducting qubits using the open SQuADDS dataset, demonstrating generalizable ML pipelines that bridge physical constraints and device geometries.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr class="agenda-item" e_utc="2026-07-27T23:00:00Z" psid="sess251" s_utc="2026-07-27T22:30:00Z" ssid="SSSN105"><td class="presentation-time-td"><span class="left-cal-links"><span style="display: none;"><!-- cal-file sess251--SSSN105.ics --><a class="ical-link" href="/wp-content/plugins/linklings_wp_program/get_cal.php?id=sess251--SSSN105"></a></span><span class="agenda-add-button agenda-button clickable" name="add_to_agenda" role="switch" style="color: #cc0000; width: 30px;" tabindex="0" title="remove from Flagged" value="remove"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" stroke-width="1.5" id="icon_linklings_minus" class="icon-linklings-minus"><g fill="none" stroke="blue" stroke-miterlimit="10"><circle cx="12" cy="12" r="10.5"></circle><path d="m6.27 12h11.46"></path></g></svg></span></span></td></tr><tr class="sess122 slots-slidedown" etypes="sstype101" style="display: none;"><td class="session-display" colspan="7"><div style="display: none;"><div class="info-section session-chairs-list-sect"><span class="info-label">Session Chairs</span><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=793843">Xiaohang Wang</a></div></span><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=999143">Fengwei Zhang</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>This session explores embedded and cross-layer security across hardware, architecture, systems, and applications. The papers span Trusted Execution Environments (TEE), microcontrollers, automotive networks, and modern CPUs, addressing both defensive mechanismsâsuch as verifiable TEE availability, memory safety, and timing mitigationâand cross-layer attacks, including side-channel analysis and adversarial AI. The session also highlights emerging challenges and opportunities in specialized acceleration, with multiple works on zero-knowledge proof, fully homomorphic encryption, and AI ASICs. Together, these papers demonstrate how security must be co-designed across layers to meet the demands of next-generation embedded and specialized computing platforms.</div><div class="info-section The presented works demonstrate how task-specific fine-tuning, reinforcement learning alignment, multi-agent reasoning, and benchmark-driven evaluation are transforming hardware design automation into a more scalable, reliable, and human-aligned process. Together, these papers chart a path toward intelligent, self-improving EDA workflows that move from prompt to silicon.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span style="display: none;"><td class="session-display" colspan="7"><div style="display: none;"><div class="info-section session-chairs-list-sect"><span class="info-label">Session Chair</span><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=716843">Chirag Goyal</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>This session focuses on innovations that affect system and software deployment across the silicon lifecycle, from early validation to in-field operation. A common theme is the impact of firmware and software on system behavior, efficiency, and correctness. Example topics are firmware-driven verification to shift validation earlier in the design process, virtualization and acceleration techniques for faster software bring-up, and automated testing in emulation environments enhanced by on-premises LLM-based failure analysis.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr class="agenda-item" This session features new low-bit quantization/formats, MoE acceleration (including speculative decoding and pruning), and KV-cache management/retrieval for long-context and streaming workloads. Together, the papers deliver a clear blueprint for faster and cheaper LLM inference in both interactive and batched settings.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span
